quotes.quote: QSO 40 This QSO (z = 0.2335 ) has a very close companion galaxy (labeled B, = 20.0 ) at 27 kpc projected distance (PD). Our optical spectrum shows prominent (EW = 63 ) [ OIII ] 5007 emission line and also very strong [ OII ] 3727 (EW = 71 ). In addition we also detect emission of [ NeIII ] 3869 and H _ and Ca II absorptions. All these lines correspond to the redshift z = 0.2332 that is identical within the measurement errors ( V = 150 km/s) to that of the QSO. This is thus a clear case of physical association QSO-close companion galaxy with evidence of significant star formation.

gmw_00003046.name: QSO 62
gmw_00003046.overview: QSO 62 is a quasar located at a redshift of z = 0.2674 [arXiv:1612.04661]. It has a quasi-edge-on spiral companion at a projected distance of 67 kpc with a discrepant redshift, as well as a fainter compact companion located 16 kpc away [arXiv:1612.04661].

sources.summary: The paper finds QSO 62 has a quasi-edge-on spiral companion at a discrepant redshift and a fainter compact companion only marginally detected.

quotes.quote: QSO 62 The QSO (z= 0.2674) has a quasi edge on spiral galaxy (labeled A, = 19.7) at PD = 67 kpc. The spectrum of this companion is characterized only by absorption features (Ca II, G band, H _ ) at z = 0.2868. The large ( 6000 km/s) difference of radial velocity excludes a present association with the quasar. At 2.5 arcsecs (corresponding to PD = 16 kpc) from the QSO there is a fainter ( = 21.1) compact companion (labeled B, see Figure fig:ima ) that is only marginally detected by our spectrum.

gmw_00003047.name: QSO 92
gmw_00003047.overview: QSO 92 is a quasar located at a redshift of z = 0.1681 [arXiv:1612.04661]. It has an associated companion galaxy, designated galaxy C, which lies at a projected distance of 72 kiloparsecs and exhibits moderate [OII] emission [arXiv:1612.04661].

sources.summary: The paper reports three nearby galaxies around QSO 92, finding only galaxy C associated with the quasar and showing moderate [OII] emission.

quotes.quote: QSO 92 In the immediate environment of this QSO (z = 0.1681) there are two similar galaxies at PD = 72 kpc (B; = 18.1) and PD = 87 kpc (C = 18.7). Our spectra shows that only galaxy C can be associated to the QSO having a difference of radial velocity of 230 km/s. The other companion galaxy (B) is a foreground object (z = 0.1175). Another fainter and closer compact companion (A; see Figure fig:ima ) is instead a background object at z 0.3. Also in this case the associated companion galaxy (C) in addition to absorption lines from the stellar population of the galaxy shows also moderate [ OII ] 3727 emission line (EW = 4.6 ).

gmw_00003048.name: QSO 130
gmw_00003048.overview: QSO 130 is a quasar located at a redshift of z = 0.1861 [arXiv:1612.04661]. It has two close companion galaxies at projected distances of 22 and 35 kiloparsecs, as well as a third companion galaxy located 82 kiloparsecs to the west [arXiv:1612.04661].

sources.summary: The paper describes QSO 130 as having multiple same-redshift companion galaxies, including one with prominent [OII], [OIII], and hydrogen emission.

quotes.quote: QSO 130 There are two close companions (A,C) at PD of 22 and 35 kpc, respectively. Both companions are at the same redshift ( V 50 km/s ) as the QSO (z = 0.1861). While companion A has a pure absorption spectrum, the companion galaxy C shows a prominent [ OII 3727 ] emission (EW = 30 ) and also [ OIII ] 5007 and H _ (see Figure fig:ima ). Curiously we found another emission line galaxy at the same redshift at 19.5 arcsecs W of the QSO (PD = 82 kpc) in the spectrum obtained with the slit oriented along the QSO-gal. A direction (E-W).

gmw_00003049.name: QSO 192
gmw_00003049.overview: QSO 192 is a quasar located at a redshift of 0.2519 [arXiv:1612.04661]. It exists within a complex immediate environment that includes two companion galaxies at very similar redshifts [arXiv:1612.04661].

sources.summary: The paper describes QSO 192 as having a complex immediate environment, with one close same-redshift galaxy and another same-redshift companion among additional objects.

quotes.quote: QSO 192 The immediate environment around this QSO (z= 0.2519 ) is rather complex (see Figure fig:ima ). There are two close companions the closest at 3.5 arcsecs W is a star and the other is a galaxy (labeled B) at 6 arcsecs NW at a redshift very close to that of the QSO (z=0.2511). We observed this field at two PA (15 ^ and 120 ^ ) obtaining the spectra of 3 more close objects (A, C and D) all are galaxies, A and C are foreground objects, while D has a redshift close to the QSO (z= 0.2513).

gmw_00003050.name: QSO 342
gmw_00003050.overview: QSO 342 is a quasar host galaxy located at a redshift of z=0.1705 [arXiv:1612.04661]. Its optical spectrum reveals an old stellar population characterized by absorption features such as Ca II, the G band, and Mg I [arXiv:1612.04661].

sources.summary: The paper finds QSO 342 has foreground companion galaxies in projection, while the host spectrum shows old-stellar absorption features.

quotes.quote: QSO 342 There are two companion galaxies (see Figure fig:ima ) that encompass this QSO (z=0.1705) at PD = 123 (A) and PD= 82 (B) . Either these companions are foreground emission line galaxies at z = 0.1297 and z = 0.0273, respectively. Our optical spectrum was secured along the direction (PA = 142^ ) connecting the companion galaxies A and B (see Figure fig:ima ). With such position we are also able to intersect the flux of the host galaxy of the quasar (at 1.3 arcsecs from its center).
The spectrum of the QSO host (see Figure fig:ima ). We detect stellar absorption lines as H,K of Ca II, G band, MgI 5875 , Ca+Fe.

gmw_00003051.name: QSO 358
gmw_00003051.overview: QSO 358 is a quasar [arXiv:1612.04661] and narrow-line Seyfert 1 galaxy [arXiv:1204.3162] hosted by a spiral galaxy [arXiv:1612.04661, arXiv:1204.3162]. The host galaxy features a tidal tail [arXiv:1612.04661] and exhibits optical, infrared, and X-ray variability [arXiv:astro-ph/0408123].

sources.summary: The paper identifies SDSS J030639.57+000343.1 as a BWB quasar, finds a strong broad-line/continuum luminosity correlation, and reports a negative Baldwin effect likely tied to host contamination.

quotes.quote: The correlations are illustrated in Fig. fig:indexgood with two examples, SDSS J030639.57+000343.1 (BWB, r_ s =-0.89 at >99.5 ), and SDSS J031022.10+004130.0 (RWB, r_ s =0.67 at 91 ).

As an example, the correlation between the broad luminosity and the continuum luminosity at 5100 is plotted in Fig. fig:LLgood for SDSS J030639.57+000343.1. A significant correlation is found with r_ s = 0.867 at confidence level of 99.7 (see also Guo Gu 2014). The linear fit gives log L_ H = (1.03 0.13) log ( L_ , 5100 ) (2.83 5.81).

Interestingly, we also found a strong positive correlation between the EW and continuum luminosity (i.e. negative Baldwin effect) in SDSS J030639.57+000343.1 (broad H , see Fig. 7) at 99 confidence level. This however is most likely caused by increasing host contamination toward fainter luminosities .

sources.summary: The paper presents XMM-Newton spectroscopy and multiwavelength counterparts for SDSS J 030639.57+000343.2, including soft and hard X-ray excesses plus optical/X-ray variability.

quotes.quote: Two SDSS sources, SDSS J 141519.50-003021.6 ( z=0.135 ) and SDSS J 030639.57+000343.2 ( z=0.107 ), turned out to be in the XMM-Newton observed fields. The distance of the sources from the boresight was 603'' and 170'' , respectively. For the sake of simplicity, they will be thereafter called SD1 and SD2, respectively.

For this source, in addition to the counterparts in the 2MASS and USNO B1 catalog, a radio counterpart was also found in the FIRST http://sundog.stsci.edu/ and NVSS http://www.cv.nrao.edu/nvss/ surveys, both at 1.4 GHz ( 20 cm). This galaxy appears to be very bright in the optical/infrared wavelengths: the 2MASS catalog reports a clear detection with all filters: J=15.08 0.08 , H=14.12 0.08 , K=13.33 0.05 . Specifically, it is worth noting the change of about 2 magnitudes in the B band between the two reference periods.

sources.summary: The paper describes QSO 358 as hosted by a spiral with a tidal tail and an edge-on companion whose velocity suggests past interaction rather than binding.

quotes.quote: QSO 358 QSO (z=0.1074) hosted by a spiral galaxy with a tidal tail. We tool the spectrum of the relatively bright (=17.6) edge-on companion galaxy that is at 133 kpc from the QSO in the direction of the extension of the tidal tail (see Figure fig:ima ). We found a pure absorption line spectrum for this companion that is at z=0.1058. The difference in terms of radial velocity between the companion and the QSO is 500 km/s thus it is unlikely that they form a bound system but suggests that there was some interaction in the past.

sources.summary: The paper describes SDSS J030639.57+000343.1 as an NLS1 with spiral morphology, possible group membership, and redshifted [OIII] emission indicating complex narrow-line-region kinematics.

quotes.quote: SDSS J030639.57+000343.1 (7): This NLS1 has a spiral morphology. The galaxy is possibly a member of a group, with a projected distance of about 110 kpc to the nearest neighbor. It has a redshifted [ OIII ] 5007 emission line ( 46 km s ^ 1 ), which, as discussed above, indicates for a complex narrow-line region kinematics 2005AJ....130..381B .

gmw_00003052.name: Grus II
gmw_00003052.overview: Grus II is an ultra-faint satellite of the Milky Way that is classified as a likely dwarf galaxy [arXiv:1911.08493]. It travels on a tightly bound orbit with an eccentricity of 0.45 and a pericenter of 25 kiloparsecs [arXiv:1911.08493].

sources.summary: The paper measures Grus II spectroscopy, velocity, metallicity, proper motion, and orbit, placing a dispersion and mass upper limit and classifying it as a likely dwarf galaxy.

quotes.quote: We present Magellan/IMACS spectroscopy of three recently discovered ultra-faint Milky Way satellites, Grus II, Tucana IV, and Tucana V. We measure systemic velocities of v_ hel = -110.0 0.5 , v_ hel = 15.9^ +1.8 _ 1.7 , and v_ hel = -36.2^ +2.5 _ 2.2 for the three objects, respectively. Their large relative velocities demonstrate that the satellites are unrelated despite their close physical proximity. We determine a velocity dispersion for Tuc IV of = 4.3^ +1.7 _ 1.0 , but we cannot resolve the velocity dispersions of the other two systems. For Gru II we place an upper limit (90 \ confidence) on the dispersion of < 1.9 , and for Tuc V we do not obtain any useful limits.
Gru II is on a tightly bound orbit with a pericenter of 25^ +6 _ 7 kpc and orbital eccentricity of 0.45^ +0.08 _ 0.05 .
Based on their sizes, masses, and metallicities, we classify Gru II and Tuc IV as likely dwarf galaxies, but the nature of Tuc V remains uncertain.

gmw_00003053.name: Tucana IV
gmw_00003053.overview: Tucana IV is an ultra-faint Milky Way satellite classified as a likely dwarf galaxy [arXiv:1911.08493]. It experienced a collision with the Large Magellanic Cloud approximately 120 million years ago [arXiv:1911.08493], and its stellar population may suffer from Small Magellanic Cloud contamination [arXiv:2312.07279].

sources.summary: The paper measures Tucana IV spectroscopy, dispersion, metallicity, proper motion, and orbit, finding a likely dwarf galaxy recently deflected by an LMC collision.

quotes.quote: We present Magellan/IMACS spectroscopy of three recently discovered ultra-faint Milky Way satellites, Grus II, Tucana IV, and Tucana V. We measure systemic velocities of v_ hel = -110.0 0.5 , v_ hel = 15.9^ +1.8 _ 1.7 , and v_ hel = -36.2^ +2.5 _ 2.2 for the three objects, respectively. Their large relative velocities demonstrate that the satellites are unrelated despite their close physical proximity. We determine a velocity dispersion for Tuc IV of = 4.3^ +1.7 _ 1.0 , but we cannot resolve the velocity dispersions of the other two systems.

The current orbit of Tuc IV is similar to that of Gru II, with a pericenter of 25^ +11 _ 8 kpc and an eccentricity of 0.36^ +0.13 _ 0.06 . However, a backward integration of the position of Tuc IV demonstrates that it collided with the Large Magellanic Cloud at an impact parameter of 4 kpc 120 Myr ago, deflecting its trajectory and possibly altering its internal kinematics. Based on their sizes, masses, and metallicities, we classify Gru II and Tuc IV as likely dwarf galaxies, but the nature of Tuc V remains uncertain.

sources.summary: The paper reports three RRL far from Tucana IV but concludes their magnitudes, proper motions, and proximity to the SMC make SMC contamination likely.

quotes.quote: Tucana IV may also suffer contamination from the SMC. All the three RRL we found in this galaxy are far away from its center, at 5, 8, and 14 R_h . Those stars also have magnitudes and proper motions overlapping with the SMC (Fig. fig:SMC ), which is at an angular distance of 12 9 from the UFD. It is very possible that these three stars are also SMC distant members.

gmw_00003054.name: CIG 96
gmw_00003054.overview: CIG 96 is an isolated barred spiral galaxy that features a strong, flat-profile bar and a grand-design spiral pattern [arXiv:1208.1409]. Massive star formation is concentrated in its two spiral arms, while its gaseous envelope exhibits a strong kinematic perturbation [arXiv:1208.1409, arXiv:0909.2736].

sources.summary: The paper studies NGC 864 as an isolated barred spiral, mapping its rotation, residual velocities, flat strong bar, grand-design arms, and arm-concentrated massive star formation.

quotes.quote: We present a study of the kinematics of the isolated spiral galaxy NGC 864, using H Fabry-Perot data obtained with the Galaxy H Fabry-Perot System (GH FaS) instrument at the William Herschel Telescope in La Palma, complemented with images at 3.6 m, in the R band and in H filter, and integral-field spectroscopic data. The resulting data cubes and velocity maps allow the study of the kinematics of the galaxy, including in-depth investigations of the rotation curve, velocity moment maps, velocity residual maps, gradient maps and position-velocity diagrams. We find asymmetries in the velocity field in the bar zone, caused by non-circular motions, probably in response to the potential of the bar. We also find a flat-profile bar, in agreement with the strong bar, with the grand design spiral pattern, and with the gap between the ends of the bar and the start of the spiral arms. We quantify the rate of massive star formation, which is concentrated in the two spiral arms.

Our bulge/disc/bar 2D decomposition of the S ^4 G 3.6 m image of NGC 864 using budda yields a flat-profile bar, which is typical of strong bars ( Athanassoula2002 ) and of two-armed grand design spirals ( Elmegreen1985 ). Flat-profile bars are linked to a higher degree of exchange of angular momentum with the halo than exponential bars ( Athanassoula2003 ). Such bars can result from sharply rising rotation curves, as observed in NGC 864, and are most likely connected to the observed spatial offset between the ends of the bars and the onset of the spiral arms.

sources.summary: The paper describes CIG 96 as an isolated galaxy whose asymmetric H I profile traces a strong gaseous-envelope kinematic perturbation, likely from capture of a gaseous companion.

quotes.quote: One of the isolated galaxies with an asymmetric profile in this subsample is CIG 96 (NGC 864), whose \ synthesis imaging from the VLA has been studied in detail in 2005A&A...442..455E . The asymmetry in its \ profile is associated with a strong kinematical perturbation in the gaseous envelope of the galaxy, where on one side the decay of the rotation curve is faster than Keplerian. Although a companion is detected, no tidal tail is found, and it is probably not massive enough to cause such a perturbation. Probably we are witnessing the capture of a gaseous companion.

gmw_00003055.name: MM 18423+5938
gmw_00003055.overview: MM 18423+5938 is a sub-millimeter galaxy located at a redshift of z = 3.930 [arXiv:1203.6852]. It is strongly magnified by gravitational lensing, which resolves its continuum emission into an Einstein ring [arXiv:1203.6852].

sources.summary: The paper treats MM 18423+5938 as the z=3.930 strongly lensed SMG target, detecting ionized gas emission, resolving an Einstein ring in continuum, and finding extended line emission with a velocity gradient.

quotes.quote: Here we present secure detections of the fine-structure line in two high- z sources, the lensed quasar ( z = 3.911 ) and the sub-millimeter galaxy (SMG) ( z = 3.930 ). These sources are strongly magnified by gravitational lensing (magnification factor =4--100 for and 12--20 for ; see Egami et al. 2000, Riechers et al. 2009, Lestrade et al. 2011), and represent two of the brightest molecular emitters at this redshift, with peak flux densities of 7.3 mJy weiss07 and 33 mJy lestrade10 , respectively.

Our PdBI observations clearly resolved the continuum emission of . The Einstein ring reported in lestrade11 is clearly seen in the pure-continuum, high resolution map shown in Figure fig_mm_all ( top-left panel ). Also the emission appears clearly extended even in the lower resolution maps shown in the top-right panel of Figure fig_mm_all . The line emission shows a clear velocity gradient from North-East (red-shifted) to South-West (blue-shifted).

sources.summary: The paper notes MM 18423+5938 lies 122 degrees from the dipole and that its small formal F/F uncertainty may be artificial because its carbon spectrum is unusually Gaussian-shaped.

quotes.quote: However, MM 18423+5938 and PSS 2322+1944 are at 122 and 106 degrees from the dipole, respectively, whereas APM 08279+5255 is at 154 degrees (Fig. CvCO , middle panel), thus being in the same hemisphere as the other two sources.

However, the fact that these fits can yield ``accuracies'' to well within a single channel, gives the relatively small error bars for MM 18423+5938 (at z=3.9 and F/F = 1.8 10^ 4 , Fig. CvCO ). Specifically, 6 \ from our fit, cf. the 24 \ (half a channel) quoted by lcs+10 . Thus, an artificially small uncertainty could arise since, of all the \ spectra, this is the most Gaussian shaped (Fig. spectra ), when in reality the resolved emission may be too complex to be best fit by a single Gaussian.

gmw_00003056.overview: 1128 047 is a flat-spectrum radio galaxy located at a redshift of z=0.266 [arXiv:0812.4617]. Very Long Baseline Interferometry observations reveal that the galaxy hosts a one-sided jet structure with a predominantly transverse magnetic field [arXiv:0812.4617].

sources.summary: The paper models 1128 047 as a flat-spectrum radio galaxy with a one-sided VLBI jet; spectral-index and brightness-temperature analysis favor a predominantly transverse magnetic field.

quotes.quote: 1128 047 This flat-spectrum radio galaxy with redshift z=0.266 (3.91 pc / mas) and mass of the central object of 5.2 10^6 M _ shows one-sided jet structure (Fig. fig1 , left) which was fitted by seven distinct Gaussian components at 8.1 GHz.

Using the VLBI images at 8.1 and 12.1 GHz, we constructed spectral index distribution and its slice along the jet ridge line (Fig. fig1 , right). Since the linear polarization in this source has not been detected down to about 1 mJy level, we calculated the model brightness temperatures for all of the jet components assuming (i) parallel (Fig. fig2 , left) and (ii) perpendicular magnetic filed configuration (Fig. fig2 , right). In the latter case, the values of T^ obs _ b and T^ mod _ b agree within the errors that allows us to suggest predominantly transverse orientation of magnetic field in the jet.
Applying the shock-in-jet model to the VLBI observations we predict the predominantly transverse direction of the intrinsic magnetic field associated with the central channel of the jet in 1128 047.

gmw_00003057.overview: The active galactic nucleus 2155 152 is a flat-spectrum source located at a redshift of z=0.672 [arXiv:0812.4617]. It has been classified as both a BL Lacertae object and a quasar based on its optical spectrum [arXiv:0812.4617].

sources.summary: The paper treats 2155 152 as a flat-spectrum BL Lac/quasar whose J2 jet component shows a brightness-temperature discrepancy explainable by particle reacceleration or a changing viewing angle.

quotes.quote: 2155 152 This flat-spectrum source with redshift z=0.672 (7.03 pc / mas) and mass of the central object of 3.9 10^7 M _ was classified as a BL Lac object by Craine et al. and then as a quasar by V eron-Cetty V eron on the basis of its optical spectrum. Using the parsec-scale spectral index information (Fig. fig3 , right) taken from the I-maps at 8.1 and 15.4 GHz (Fig. fig3 , left) together with the results from model fitting, and also assuming magnetic field to be perpendicular to the jet, we have calculated the values of T^ mod _ b and T^ obs _ b . The brightness temperatures predicted by the model agree well with those from the data for all the components (Fig. fig4 , right), except the optically thin J2 feature ( _ J2 =-0.75 ) located at 3.36 mas from the core, for which =T^ obs _ b /T^ mod _ b 20 .

Discrepancy between the model and measured brightness temperatures for the bright jet component, located at 3.4 mas from the core in 2155 152, can be explained either by reacceleration of the particles due to the interaction with the ambient medium or by changing the viewing angle that becomes nearly zero.

gmw_00003058.name: BCG of the Phoenix cluster
aliases_local: central galaxy of the Phoenix cluster
central galaxy in the Phoenix cluster
aliases_external: BCG/central AGN of SPT-CL J2344-4243 (Phoenix cluster)
gmw_00003058.overview: The central galaxy of the Phoenix cluster is a massive giant elliptical galaxy [arXiv:1611.00017, arXiv:1904.08942] hosting an obscured active galactic nucleus [arXiv:1211.7058, arXiv:1908.07546] that drives relativistic radio jets [arXiv:1611.00017, arXiv:1912.07853]. It is experiencing an extreme starburst fueled by a massive cooling flow [arXiv:1211.7058, arXiv:1311.0867], which is sustained by a large reservoir of cold molecular gas [arXiv:1611.00017, arXiv:1904.08942].

sources.original_name: central galaxy in the Phoenix cluster
sources.summary: The paper describes the Phoenix cluster central galaxy as a starbursting BCG with a powerful AGN, extended UV filaments, young stars, and a cooling-flow-fueled star formation rate near 800 solar masses per year.

quotes.quote: The central galaxy in the Phoenix cluster appears to be experiencing a 740 M _ yr ^ 1 starburst. However, it also harbors a powerful AGN which makes it difficult to separate contributions to the ultraviolet, optical, and infrared emission from star-forming regions versus the AGN in low-resolution imaging. Further, the available ground-based data optical and space-based ultraviolet (UV) and infrared (IR) data presented in were unable to distinguish between in situ star formation or the late stages of a merger.

The high spatial resolution of HST is able to separate bright UV emission from the AGN and the surrounding diffuse, extended emission, definitively confirming the presence of a starburst in the BCG. The morphology of this central galaxy is complex, with narrow filaments extending for > 40 kpc, reminiscent of the nearby Perseus and Abell 1795 clusters. We argue that the majority of the observed UV emission is due to young stars, on the basis of the complex morphology and flat SED over the wavelength range 1000--5500 .

We confirm the high SFR presented in , measuring an extinction-corrected, AGN-removed UV-derived SFR of 798 42 M yr ^ 1 . We find that merger driven scenarios would require an unreasonably large number of gas rich galaxies to supply the cold gas reservoir required to fuel the starburst, and conclude that the starburst is likely fueled by a massive cooling flow.

aliases_in_paper: central galaxy in the Phoenix cluster
sources.summary: The paper describes Phoenix A as an extreme starburst BCG with efficient cooling-flow star formation, unusual molecular-gas use, strong line emission, and likely AGN or shock contributions.
sources.paper_coordinate_text: The pointing center was chosen to be at the center of the BCG, = 23^h44^m43 96 , = -42 43 12 2 (J2000)

quotes.quote: Unlike nearby ``cool core clusters'' -- characterized by dense, cool central regions -- which typically convert only a few percent of the cooling intracluster medium (ICM) into stars e.g., johnstone87,mcnamara89,odea08,mcdonald11b , the central galaxy in the Phoenix cluster (hereafter Phoenix A) appears to be experiencing an 800 M _ yr ^ 1 starburst mcdonald13a - consuming roughly 30--40 of the expected cooling flow. This estimate of the star formation rate in Phoenix A, which is based on Hubble Space Telescope far-ultraviolet imaging, is in line with the expected efficiency of star formation in giant molecular clouds 20--50 ; kroupa01a,lada03 , suggesting that the ``cooling flow problem'' for a review, see fabian94 may not be as severe in this unique system.

We show a range of values for _ CO in Phoenix A: from a ULIRG-like value of 0.4 to a Galactic value of 4.0 (see 3.4). This figure demonstrates that Phoenix A appears to be using its molecular gas more efficiently, with a relatively small cold gas reservoir given the enormous star formation rate and H luminosity. While our choice of _ CO is highly uncertain, even assuming a Galactic value (similar to and ) leads to a value of M _ H_2 for Phoenix A that lies below the extrapolation from nearby systems. In general, the low-ionization optical ( [ O ii ] , H ) emission lines in Phoenix A appear to be consistent with a ``scaling-up'' of classical low-redshift systems such as Abell 1795 and Abell 1835.

The primary difference between Phoenix A and a scaled-up, low-redshift BCG is the efficiency with which the cooling flow is converted into stars, which sits at 30 , compared to the typical low-z value of a few percent. This is also reflected in the ratio of star formation to cold molecular gas, which is much higher for Phoenix A than for nearby BCGs (regardless of our choice of _ CO ).

sources.summary: The paper links the Phoenix cluster BCG to a massive starburst, a powerful obscured AGN, and a revised Herschel SFR of 530 solar masses per year compared with cooling-flow rates.

quotes.quote: The strongest hint comes from the very high star formation rate (SFR) observed in the brightest cluster galaxy (BCG), which has originally been estimated to be 700 M_ yr ^ 1 , with large 1 errors ranging from 200 to 500 M_ yr ^ 1 2012McDonald . However, an accurate measurement of the SFR is made difficult by the presence of a strongly absorbed AGN, whose contribution to the BCG emission can be accounted for in different ways. Recently, the HST/WFC3 observation of the Phoenix 2013aMcDonald showed filamentary blue emission out to 40 kpc and beyond, and the estimated, extinction-corrected SFR has been updated to a more accurate value of 798 42 M_ yr ^ 1 , consistent with optical and IR data at lower spatial resolution. In this work we present the analysis of a 220 ks observation with XMM-Newton awarded in AO12 on the Phoenix cluster, with the main goal of investigating the thermal structure of the cool core and comparing the mass deposition rate in the core to the star formation rate in the BCG. We also use archival Chandra data (about 10 ks with ACIS-I) to model the emission of the AGN in the BCG and global ICM properties.

A careful analysis of the FIR SED based on Herschel data provided a value for the SFR in the BCG of 530 M_ yr ^ 1 with an uncertainty of 15 . This revised estimate of the SFR agrees very well with the M from EPIC MOS data and is consistent within 1 with M from the RGS analysis, while still inconsistent at more than 3 with the mass deposition rate found in EPIC pn data. Our revised SFR therefore does not significantly change the comparison between SFR and the global M in the Phoenix cluster. To summarize, the range of M allowed by XMM-Newton data from EPIC MOS is consistent with the SFR observed in the BCG, while EPIC pn and RGS data analyses suggest a mass deposition rate of about SFR/3 , and the derived upper limit is inconsistent with the observed SFR at least at the 2 level for RGS, and more than 3 for EPIC-pn.

sources.original_name: central galaxy of the Phoenix cluster
sources.summary: The paper studies the Phoenix cluster central galaxy, whose starburst, molecular gas reservoir, radio bubbles, quasar radiation, and jets show strong coupling between cold gas and AGN feedback.

quotes.quote: We report new ALMA observations of the CO(3-2) line emission from the 2.1 0.3 10^ 10 molecular gas reservoir in the central galaxy of the Phoenix cluster. The cold molecular gas is fuelling a vigorous starburst at a rate of 500-800 and powerful black hole activity in the form of both intense quasar radiation and radio jets. The radio jets have inflated huge bubbles filled with relativistic plasma into the hot, X-ray atmospheres surrounding the host galaxy.

Here we present new ALMA observations of the CO(3-2) emission from the molecular gas in the central galaxy of the Phoenix cluster. Discovered with the South Pole Telescope, the Phoenix cluster (SPT-CLJ2344-4243), at redshift z=0.596 , is the most luminous X-ray cluster known ( Williamson11,McDonald12 ), and the 500-800 starburst hosted by its central galaxy is amongst the largest found in any galaxy below redshift 1. The star formation is observed in bright filaments stretching beyond 100 , sustained by a 20 billion solar mass reservoir of molecular gas ( McDonald13,McDonald14 ). The stellar mass of the massive central galaxy is 3 10^ 12 ( McDonald12,McDonald13 ) and it hosts an unusual central supermassive black hole that is powering both intense radiation and relativistic jets.

sources.summary: The paper interprets the Phoenix central galaxy’s extreme star formation as a short cooling-phase episode, with molecular gas from cooling and an AGN outburst not yet quenching it.

quotes.quote: Recent observations show that the star formation rate (SFR) in the Phoenix cluster's central galaxy is 500 M _ yr ^ 1 . Even though Phoenix is a massive cluster ( M_ 200 2.0 10^ 15 M _ ; z 0.6 ) such a high central SFR is not expected in a scenario in which feedback from an active galactic nucleus (AGN) maintains the intracluster medium (ICM) in a state of rough thermal balance.

Using results from a 3D hydrodynamic simulation of a standard cool-core cluster ( M_ 200 7 10^ 14 M _ ; z=0 ), scaled to account for differences in mass and redshift, we argue that Phoenix is at the end of a cooling phase in which an AGN outburst has begun but has not yet arrested core cooling. This state of high cooling rate and star formation is expected to last for 100 Myr in Phoenix .

Phoenix has a high current SFR but its fraction of young stars indicates that it has not been forming stars at that rate for very long. The observed amount of molecular gas ( M_ cold >10^ 10 M _ ) most likely has originated in gas cooling from the surrounding hot atmosphere over 50-120 Myr ( Russell2017 ). Pure cooling for a few hundred Myr (Figure fig:tctf_color ) is enough to make the cluster look like a pure cooling flow, consistent with our interpretation of a short cooling cycle.

sources.summary: The paper describes the Phoenix cluster central BCG as a radio and AGN source embedded in minihalo emission, linking BCG radio emission and jets to X-ray cavities and possible spectral-index contamination.

quotes.quote: vanWeeren2014 discovered the presence of diffuse radio emission in the Phoenix cluster with 610 MHz observations, surrounding the central BCG with no obvious connection to the radio galaxies and classified the source as a radio minihalo. Hlavacek2015 first discovered X-ray cavities in the cluster core resulted from the AGN feedback. McDonald2015 found a connection between the X-ray cavities and radio emission from the BCG with 610 MHz GMRT observations where the radio emission appears to be oriented towards the X-ray cavities. Recently, with the VLA 8-12 GHz observations of the Phoenix cluster, McDonald2019a reported the near correspondence of these X-ray cavities with the radio jets revealing the presence of mechanical feedback from the central AGN.

It is worth pointing out that McDonald2019a reported the presence of radio jets from the central BCG in the Phoenix cluster, but our 1.52 GHz observation lacks the necessary spatial resolution to quantify the radio emission from these jets accurately. So, the possibility of jet emission contamination in the flux density estimation at 1.52 GHz, resulting in the relatively flatter spectrum, can not be ruled out. In addition, Fig. fig:residual-background shows that surprisingly, the minihalo emission extends in the direction of the BCG jets (Fig. 5 McDonald2019a ) further indicating the possible AGN influence in the ICM. Since the possible contamination by the AGN jets can not be ruled out, the estimated spectral index value should be regarded as an upper limit to the minihalo spectral index.

sources.summary: The paper identifies the Phoenix-cluster BCG as hosting a luminous SMBH and central AGN whose absorbed X-ray continuum and Fe line make it an unusual type 2 QSO case.

quotes.quote: Among them, we select the Phoenix cluster at the redshift z = 0.596 , hosting a massive BCG with very luminous SMBH.
We hence regard the most natural idea is that the Fe 1 line comes from the central AGN in the BCG of the Phoenix cluster.

The X-ray spectrum of the central AGN in the Phoenix cluster is characterized with an strongly absorbed ( = 3.2 ^ +0.9 _ 0.8 10^ 23 cm ^ 2 ) power-law continuum plus the K-shell line from a neutral iron ( Fe 1 ). The EW of the Fe 1 line ( 149 ^ +139 _ 58 eV) and the absorption column density are typical for Compton-thin type 2 AGNs. However the EW is significantly larger than that of the general trend of the X-ray Baldwin effect, extrapolated to the luminosity as high as that of the type 2 QSO in the Phoenix cluster.

sources.summary: The paper predicts the Phoenix BCG's old stellar density profile should be close to a Sersic n=4 model with a slightly steeper central cusp and sensitivity to compactness.

quotes.quote: Based on this scenario and our initial N Body setup we predict that the old/red stellar density profile of the Phoenix BCG (red curves in Figure Dens_muesli ) should resemble a classic S e rsic n=4 model which becomes slightly steeper and more cusp-like within 0.13R_ e . Interestingly, the inner most data points (within 0.1R_ e ) of the measured surface brightness profile in Figure 3 from seem to lie above the S e rsic n=4 reference model. However, note that our N Body models are not fine-tuned to exactly match the properties of the Phoenix BCG nor its redshift. A more compact size of its BCG of R_ e = 17 kpc 2013ApJ...765L..37M but otherwise identical initial parameters results in a larger initial mass/light deficit. This would necessitate even larger amounts of swallowed mass, , in order to steepen its central slope.

sources.summary: The Phoenix central galaxy is described as an extreme star-forming BCG with a 500-800 solar-mass-per-year starburst, large cool-gas reservoirs, and no merger-trigger signature.

quotes.quote: This system is the most X-ray luminous cluster known and harbors the most star-forming central galaxy known, with measurements of the star formation rate ranging from 500--800 M _ yr ^ 1 mcdonald12c,mcdonald13a,mittal17 . The high X-ray luminosity and BCG star formation rate are most likely related, as the starburst appears to be fueled by the rapid cooling (high luminosity) of the ICM mcdonald12c , implying that > 10 of the predicted cooling is ultimately realized in star formation.

In Figure fig:rgb , we see the central starburst in the Phoenix cluster at unprecedented angular resolution. For the first time, the smooth, underlying giant elliptical galaxy can be seen extending on 50 kpc scales, with no evidence for tidal disruption as one would expect if the 600 M _ yr ^ 1 starburst was triggered by a massive gas-rich merger. This central galaxy is known to have large amounts of gas at 10 ^5 K mcdonald15b , 10 ^4 K mcdonald14a , and in the molecular phase russell17 .

sources.summary: The paper keeps BAT ID 1204 as the Phoenix-cluster central obscured AGN, despite its luminosity outlier status, because its X-ray properties look non-beamed.

quotes.quote: We note that the highest luminosity source in our sample, BAT ID 1204 (2MASX J23444387 4243124, at z=0.597 ), is the AGN at the centre of the Phoenix cluster . It is an apparent outlier both in terms of its luminosity and location in the luminosity-redshift plane (see Fig. fig:Lum_vs_z ). However, we have no clear indication for this source to be beamed or otherwise different from the other AGN that pass our selection criteria, and its spectral X-ray properties are consistent with those of an accretion-powered, non-beamed, obscured AGN . We have thus decided to keep it in the sample.

gmw_00003059.name: BCG of A1139
gmw_00003059.overview: The BCG of A1139 is the brightest cluster galaxy of the Abell 1139 galaxy cluster [arXiv:1803.07223]. This galaxy is dynamically younger than the more massive, relaxed brightest cluster galaxy of Abell 2589 [arXiv:1803.07223].

sources.summary: The paper cites pCMD comparisons showing the A1139 BCG belongs to a dynamically young cluster and is dynamically younger than the more massive, relaxed A2589 BCG.

quotes.quote: They compared the brightest cluster galaxies (BCGs) in Abell 1139 (A1139) and Abell 2589 (A2589) using the pCMD analysis technique, which are two of the 14 galaxy clusters targeted in the KASI-Yonsei Deep Imaging Survey of Clusters KYDISC; oh18 . By simplifying the overall pCMD features into the mean and standard deviation of pixel color as a function of pixel surface brightness (called a pCMD backbone ), lee17 showed that the BCG of dynamically relaxed A2589 formed a larger central body at an early epoch and has grown to be a larger, more massive and dynamically better relaxed galaxy today than the BCG of dynamically young A1139.
Based on this inference, lee17 concluded that the BCG of A1139 is dynamically younger than the BCG of A2589.

gmw_00003060.name: BCG of A2589
gmw_00003060.overview: The BCG of A2589 is a dynamically relaxed, massive brightest cluster galaxy [arXiv:1803.07223]. It features a large central body that formed at an early epoch, and it contains the remnants of infalling, low-mass star-forming satellite galaxies [arXiv:1803.07223].

sources.summary: The paper describes the A2589 BCG as dynamically relaxed, larger and more massive than the A1139 BCG, with pCMD outliers interpreted as infalling star-forming satellites.

quotes.quote: They compared the brightest cluster galaxies (BCGs) in Abell 1139 (A1139) and Abell 2589 (A2589) using the pCMD analysis technique, which are two of the 14 galaxy clusters targeted in the KASI-Yonsei Deep Imaging Survey of Clusters KYDISC; oh18 . By simplifying the overall pCMD features into the mean and standard deviation of pixel color as a function of pixel surface brightness (called a pCMD backbone ), lee17 showed that the BCG of dynamically relaxed A2589 formed a larger central body at an early epoch and has grown to be a larger, more massive and dynamically better relaxed galaxy today than the BCG of dynamically young A1139.
Based on this inference, lee17 concluded that the BCG of A1139 is dynamically younger than the BCG of A2589.
For example, the BCG of A2589 (A2589-00001) is thought to have vestiges of infalling low-mass star-forming satellites, which was revealed from the analysis of its pCMD outliers in lee17 .

gmw_00003061.name: GRB 090205 host galaxy
gmw_00003061.overview: The GRB 090205 host galaxy is a starburst galaxy located at a redshift of z=4.65 [arXiv:1006.0800]. It is classified as a Lyman-alpha emitter with a young stellar population under 150 million years old [arXiv:1006.0800].

sources.summary: The paper identifies the GRB 090205 host as a z=4.65 Lyman-alpha emitter/starburst, with a young stellar population, relatively high afterglow metallicity, and the most distant spectroscopically confirmed GRB host then detected.

quotes.quote: Photometric observations of the GRB 090205 host galaxy argue in favor of a starburst galaxy with a stellar population younger than 150 Myr. Moreover, the metallicity of Z > 0.27 Z_ derived from the GRB afterglow spectrum is among the highest derived from GRB afterglow measurement at high- z , suggesting that the burst occured in a rather enriched envirorment. Finally, a detailed analysis of the afterglow spectrum shows the existence of a line corresponding to Lyman- emission at the redshift of the burst. GRB 090205 is thus hosted in a typical Lyman- emitter (LAE) at z=4.65 . This makes the GRB 090205 host the farthest GRB host galaxy, spectroscopically confirmed, detected to date.

gmw_00003062.name: ESO240-G11
gmw_00003062.overview: ESO240-G11 is an edge-on disk galaxy that exhibits an asymmetric, L-like warp [arXiv:1009.2112]. This warp is visible in optical bands but is not present in the infrared [arXiv:1009.2112].

sources.summary: The paper identifies ESO240-G11 as an edge-on disk with an L-like warp, warped in optical bands but not infrared, and showing the largest V-to-Ks warp displacement difference in the sample.

quotes.quote: The frequency of asymmetric warps is lower: ESO416-G25 exhibits a U-like warp, while ESO240-G11 and ESO555-G36 show L-like warps.

Five of our galaxies (ESO235-G53, ESO288-G25, ESO311-G12, ESO416-G25, and ESO555-G36) show similar warp curves in all four observed bands, one galaxy (ESO531-G22) is warped in all four filters but exhibits a clearly weaker IR warp, one galaxy (ESO564-G27) is warped in the V, I , and K_ s filters, and six galaxies (ESO142-G24, ESO157-G18, ESO201-G22, ESO202-G35, ESO240-G11, and ESO340-G08) are warped in the optical bands but not in the IR.
The most extreme case is ESO240-G11 (right-hand side), where the y(V)-z(K_ s ) = 5.5 .

gmw_00003063.name: The host of the absorber towards SDSSJ111008.61 + 024458.0
aliases_local: The host of the absorber towards SDSSJ111008.61 + 024458.0
gmw_00003063.overview: The host galaxy of the absorber towards SDSSJ111008.61+024458.0 is located 17 kiloparsecs north of its background quasar [arXiv:1107.4618]. It has a dust-corrected star formation rate of 19 solar masses per year and a velocity field indicative of a merger system [arXiv:1107.4618].

sources.original_name: The host of the absorber towards SDSSJ111008.61 + 024458.0
sources.summary: Detected as an absorber host north of its QSO, this galaxy has a measured star-formation rate and a velocity field interpreted as a merger rather than a system with an assigned dynamical mass.

quotes.quote: Out of our SINFONI observations towards \ \ absorbers, we detect \ emission of only 3 galaxies (towards SDSSJ111008.61 + 024458.0, SDSSJ125525.67 + 030518.4 and Q2243 60).

Figure fig:maps (a-) shows the flux, velocity and dispersion maps of the 3 fields (SDSSJ111008.61 + 024458.0, SDSSJ125525.67 + 030518.4 and Q2243 60, respectively) towards which we could detect the host. The host of the absorber towards SDSSJ111008.61 + 024458.0 is found only 17kpc North from the QSO.

The absorber at z=2.1187 has =2.97 0.17 \ MurphyM_04c,ProchaskaJ_05a and is found to have a SFR =9.1 \ for a Chabrier IMF, corresponding to a dust-corrected SFR _0 of 19 . Its velocity field is indicative of a merger system with a reversal of the velocity field, hence we refrain ourselves in assigning a dynamical mass.

gmw_00003064.name: The host of the absorber towards SDSSJ125525.67 + 030518.4
aliases_local: The host of the absorber towards SDSSJ125525.67 + 030518.4
gmw_00003064.overview: The host galaxy of the absorber towards SDSSJ125525.67+030518.4 is located 14 kpc from the QSO [arXiv:1107.4618]. It exhibits a modest star-formation rate [arXiv:1107.4618] and a velocity field with a clear rotation pattern [arXiv:1107.4618].

sources.original_name: The host of the absorber towards SDSSJ125525.67 + 030518.4
sources.summary: This absorber host lies 14 kpc from the QSO, has a modest star-formation rate, shows a rotating velocity field, and yields dynamical and halo mass estimates from its half-light radius.

quotes.quote: The host of the absorber towards SDSSJ125525.67 + 030518.4 with =3.20 0.23 \ MurphyM_04c,ProchaskaJ_05a is found at 14kpc from the QSO. The host has a SFR =8.6 \ calculated also assuming a Chabrier IMF, corresponding to a dust-corrected SFR _0 of 18 .
The velocity field shows a clear rotation pattern of v_ max i 70 \ with a dispersion that peaks at 70 \ in the center. The intrinsic dispersion is _0 30 \ in the outskirts.

The dynamical mass within the half-light radius (0.44 ) is M_ 1/2 2.0 10^ 10 , taking into account the inclination i=24^ estimated from the axis ratio b/a=0.9 . Using v_ max , our estimate of the haloe mass is 2.5 10^ 11 .

gmw_00003065.name: The host towards Q2243 60
gmw_00003065.overview: The host galaxy towards Q2243 60 is a high-redshift star-forming galaxy with a dust-corrected star formation rate of 77 solar masses per year [arXiv:1107.4618]. Its velocity field exhibits a rotation pattern with a maximum velocity of approximately 100 kilometers per second and a large central dispersion [arXiv:1107.4618].

sources.summary: The paper describes the Q2243 60 absorber host as the strongest line-flux detection, with a rotating velocity field, adaptive-optics-resolved kinematics, and dynamical and halo mass estimates.

quotes.quote: The host towards Q2243 60 has the highest \ flux in our sample, reaching almost 1 10^ 16 , corresponding to an observed Using /Hb , we find, in Bouch e et al. (in preparation), that E(B-V) 0.6 and therefore the dust-corrected SFR is SFR _0 of 77 . SFR =18 .

The velocity field also shows a rotation pattern with a large central dispersion 120 . This galaxy is less resolved (its half-light radius 0.5 \ is similar to the PSF 0.6 ). We recently obtained SINFONI observations (in H and K ) with adaptive optics (AO) for this field that will be presented elsewhere (Bouch e et al. in preparation).

We can already report that the velocity field is resolved with v_ max i 100 , the intrinsic dispersion is _0 100 . With an inclination of 57 2 ^ , the dynamical mass within the half-light radius (0.45 ) is M_ 1/2 1.8 10^ 10 . The halo mass is estimated at 2 10^ 11 \ from v_ max .

gmw_00003066.name: 4C +28.07
gmw_00003066.overview: The active galactic nucleus 4C +28.07 is a flat-spectrum radio quasar [arXiv:2107.10555, arXiv:2311.15325] located at a redshift of 1.206 [arXiv:2107.10555, arXiv:2311.15325]. It exhibits a parsec-scale radio jet that bends sharply [arXiv:1101.5822] and displays multiwavelength variability, including correlated optical and gamma-ray flares [arXiv:1404.5967, arXiv:2403.13713].

sources.summary: The paper reports optical and near-infrared flaring of B2 0234+28, MAGIC follow-up during optical activity, and 25.6 hours of collected observations.

quotes.quote: B2 0234+28 ( z=1.206 , 2012ApJ...748...49S ): In October 2018, the Special Astrophysical Observatory of the Russian Academy of Sciences (SAO RAS) reported a new active phase of the source, which increased its flux in the R band by a factor of 3 magnitudes with respect to its quiet state 2018ATel12111....1V . The Guillermo Haro Observatory observed a flare in NIR on 5th January 2019. They reported that the source had increased its flux by 50 2019ATel12401....1C . The increase in the flux level happened on a daily timescale.

MAGIC observed the source in 2018 and 2019 during its increased activity in the optical band. The source reached the highest flux in October 2018 which was 6--10 times higher than the average flux observed by Fermi LAT. KVA and Fermi LAT LCs are shown in Fig.  fig:plot_mwl_b20234 , covering the time of MAGIC observation. MAGIC followed this source at that time and collected 25.6 h of data.

sources.summary: The paper models 4C+28.07 as an FSRQ monitored across wavelengths, finding three gamma-ray flaring states, radio-gamma delays, and a two-zone leptonic jet model.

quotes.quote: 4C+28.07 is a ray Flat Spectrum Radio Quasar (FSRQ) type source. It is often monitored at different frequencies, though long term multi-wavelength data of this source have not been modelled in detail before. We have analyzed 12 years (Aug, 2008 - May, 2020) of Fermi-LAT data with a binning of 10 days time scale and observed three distinctive flaring states. Each flaring state consists of different phases of activity, namely, pre-flare, flare post-flare regions.

4C+28.07 is a FSRQ (Flat Spectrum Radio Quasars) type and moderately variable extra-galactic source with RA: 2h37m52.406s, DEC: +28d48m8.990s 2004AJ....127.3587F , located at red-shift 1.206 2012ApJ...748...49S frequently monitored by Fermi Large Area Telescope (Fermi-LAT) since 2008. It was observed in radio with Effelsberg 100 m and the IRAM 30 m telescope since January and June 2007. It reached a high state in radio in mid 2008, subsequently the flux was decreasing until 2010, when the source reached the minima in radio flux 2011ATel.3674....1N .

sources.summary: The paper analyzes 4C +28.07 as an FSRQ blazar, using multifrequency VLBI and gamma-ray data to study spectral variability and synchrotron self-absorption magnetic fields.

quotes.quote: We present the analysis results of the simultaneous multifrequency observations of the blazar 4C +28.07. The observations were conducted by the Interferometric Monitoring of Gamma-ray Bright Active Galactic Nuclei (iMOGABA) program, which is a key science program of the Korean Very Long Baseline Interferometry (VLBI) Network (KVN). Observations of the iMOGABA program for 4C +28.07 were conducted from 16 January 2013 (MJD 56308) to 13 March 2020 (MJD 58921).

4C +28.07 is a FSRQ fey2004second ( z = 1.206 ) with a SMBH mass of M_ BH = (1.65_ 0.82 ^ +1.66 ) 10^ 9 M _ shaw2012spectroscopy, zargaryan2022multiwavelength . Multiwavelength observational studies of 4C +28.07 have mainly revealed the physical properties of ray flares from the source. das2021multi conducted a long-period cross-correlation study of 4C +28.07, analyzing multi-wavelength ( ray to radio) data obtained from the Fermi-Large Area Telescope Fermi-LAT; atwood2009large , Swift-XRT/UVOT gehrels2004swift, burrows2005swift, roming2005swift , Catalina surveys drake2009first , the Steward Optical Observatory smith2009coordinated , and from the Owens Valley Radio Observatory OVRO; richards2011blazars .

sources.summary: The paper describes a quasar with a pc-scale VLBI jet, a bent VLA jet, clear X-ray emission to the sharp bend, and marginal X-ray emission near the terminal radio feature.

quotes.quote: 0234 + 285 (4C +28.07) On the pc scale,VLBI observations show two jet knots with PAs of about -15 \ and apparent speeds of about 12 2009AJ....138.1874L . The VLA image shows a jet about 6 \ long with an initial direction of due north but curving to a PA of -20 \ after which it bends sharply to a PA of -90 , and terminates within 3 \ at a bright component. X-ray emission is clearly detected up to the sharp bend. There is a marginal X-ray detection near the radio feature at the end of the detected jet.

sources.summary: The study highlights 4C +28.07 as the clearest correlated FSRQ, with nearly simultaneous optical and gamma-ray flares and a DCF gamma-ray lead of about four days.

quotes.quote: From the light curves, perhaps the clearest correlation among these sources (although not the most significant) is in the FSRQ 4C +28.07, with nearly simultaneous gamma-ray and optical flares at MJD 55,900. The DCF peak for 4C +28.07 indicates a gamma-ray lead of = 4.1 1.3 days. Visually, the good correlation in the brightest flares supports this short time scale, but the DCF peak width at half maximum is 80 days. This likely represents the characteristic width of the flaring episodes, but may also describe a variation in the true lead/lag.

sources.summary: The paper describes 0234+285 as showing short-term multifrequency variability, a declining millimetre trend after an early-2012 flare, no gamma-ray/millimetre correlation, and significant millimetre-band lag structure.

quotes.quote: 0234+285 : The multifrequency light curve of the source is characterized by short-term variations with decreasing trend from the major flare that occurred during early 2012 in the millimetre band, and one flare in the ray band. The ray flare was by a factor of 3 smaller than the one that occurred toward the end of 2011 (see Paper I). No significant correlations between the ray and millimetre bands were obtained, unlike the 60 d lag reported in Paper I. The cross-correlations within the millimetre bands were significant with a time lag of 6 d with the 95 GHz preceding the 37 GHz light curve.

gmw_00003067.name: PKS 0454 234
gmw_00003067.overview: PKS 0454 234 is a flat-spectrum radio quasar [arXiv:1807.02085, arXiv:2011.01073] that exhibits rapid gamma-ray variability, with flare timescales indicating emission from extremely compact jet regions [arXiv:2011.01073]. The source also displays orphan-like gamma-ray flares with minimal optical activity, followed by delayed optical flaring [arXiv:1404.5967].

sources.summary: The paper uses Mg II line measurements for J0456.9 2323 to derive disk and black-hole parameters, then models its time-averaged FSRQ SED with leptonic and hadronic scenarios.

quotes.quote: We have collected the optical spectroscopic emission line parameters from the literature to derive both M_ BH and L_ disk . We take the Mg ii line luminosity of J0456.9 2323 and J1427.3 4204 from 1993A&AS..100..395S and 1989A&AS...80..103S respectively. By following the empirical relation and line coefficients of 2012ApJ...748...49S , we derive both L_ disk and M_ BH . In particular, L_ disk is computed following the scaling relations of 1991ApJ...373..465F and 1997MNRAS.286..415C and assuming that the BLR reprocesses 10 of L_ disk .

We generate steady-state, i.e. time-averaged, broadband SEDs of all 8 FSRQs following the details outlined in Section sec3 and reproduce them using both leptonic and lepto-hadronic emission scenarios. The leptonic model SEDs are presented in Figure fig_leptonic_sed and the associated SED parameters are given in Table tab:leptonic_sed_param . The results of the lepto-hadronic SED modeling are shown in Figure fig_hadronic_sed and we provide the physical parameters derived from the modeling in Table tab:hadronic_sed_param . Using the results of the SED modeling, we compute the degree of X-ray and ray polarization predicted by the leptonic and hadronic models.

sources.summary: The paper finds compact PKS 0454-234 flare regions but inconclusive spectral and cooling tests, while VHE photon evidence suggests emission beyond the BLR.

quotes.quote: PKS 0454-234 PKS 0454-234 is seen to have a shortest variability timescale of 1.62 0.28 hours for the January 2009 flare and 1.39 0.24 hours during the November 2011 flare. Both timescales indicate emission from extremely compact regions in the jet of size (4.55 0.79) 10^ 13 m and (3.91 0.67) 10^ 13 m respectively. However, search for evidence of a possible cut-off in the spectrum for both flares proved inconclusive. This was also the case for the investigation into the energy dependence in cooling timescales, due to the large uncertainties in flux.

While the flare intervals studied are both accompanied by VHE emission, the most energetic VHE photon ( E = 185.9 14.2 GeV) was observed on MJD 57486.05, when there is no evidence for any flaring activity at lower energies. An energy cut-off at 27.5 2.4 GeV best agrees with this observation; this is significantly higher than the 12.5 GeV expected for this source due to interaction with Lyman alpha photons and suggests emission from beyond the BLR.

sources.summary: The paper reports low-flux crude time-resolution periods for PKS 0454-234, sub-hour gamma-ray variability limits, and PSD slopes consistent with NM13 within errors.

quotes.quote: On the other hand, light curves of PKS 0454-234, 3C 454, and 3C 273 contain time periods with crude time resolution corresponding to the low flux states of the sources reflected by vertical rises in Figure fig:dt , which separate periods of fine sub-day time resolution associated with the high flux states. The upper limits on the short characteristic time scales obtained in our studies hint toward a sub-hour ray time variability in at least 4 blazars: B2 1633+38, B2 1520+31, PKS 0454-234, and 3C 273, all belonging to the FSRQ class (Figure fig:results , left). We found that in PKS 0454-234, 3C 279, 3C 66A, and PKS 2155-304 the two methods are consistent with each other in terms of the derived PSD slopes within the provided error bars.

sources.summary: PKS 0454 234 shows orphan-like gamma-ray flares with little optical activity, followed by a later optical flare that may imply an unusual delay needing longer monitoring.

quotes.quote: We see several such flares, such as in the light curve of the FSRQ PKS 0454 234 in Figure 0457 , at MJD 55,850, where several strong gamma-ray flares show little optical activity nearby in time. The next large optical flare peaks at MJD 56,000; if it is the counterpart of the gamma-ray activity, it suggests a unique 150 day delay (the alternative is that this optical flare is also an orphan).
In our case of PKS 0454 234 and others like it, substantially longer light curves or more multi-wavelength data are needed to confirm or dismiss a causal origin of such large delays.

gmw_00003068.name: SDSS J211852.96-073227.5
gmw_00003068.overview: SDSS J211852.96-073227.5 is an intermediate-type Seyfert galaxy [arXiv:2004.07092] hosted by a late-type galaxy [arXiv:2004.07092] that is currently merging with a nearby companion [arXiv:2004.02703, arXiv:2004.07092]. The active nucleus hosts a young, closely aligned relativistic jet [arXiv:2004.02703] and exhibits rapid infrared variability [arXiv:1911.03500].

sources.summary: The paper reports TXS 2116 077 as a redshift 0.26 NLSy1 with a beamed relativistic jet, compact radio and X-ray emission, and an ongoing minor merger.
sources.paper_coordinate_text: Right Ascension: 21 ^ h 18 ^ m 52.96 ^ s and Declination, J2000 = 07 ^ 32 ^ 27.58 ^

quotes.quote: Here we report observations of TXS 2116 077, a NLSy1 galaxy 2017ApJS..229...39R with redshift z=0.26 corresponding to a distance of 1329 Mpc; 2016A&A...594A..13P . TXS 2116 077 possesses a relativistic jet closely aligned to our line of sight as confirmed by the detection of its variable ray flux, the observation of a compact radio core at 8.4 GHz, its large radio-loudness, and flat radio and X-ray spectra 2018ApJ...853L...2P,2018MNRAS.477.5127Y . The broadband spectral energy distribution of TXS 2116 077 resembles that of blazars 2018MNRAS.477.5127Y,2019ApJ...872..169P , which again confirms the presence of a closely aligned relativistic jet in this NLSy1 galaxy.

We have carried out, for the first time, a high-resolution, adaptive optics enabled, J band imaging of the host galaxy of TXS 2116 077 with 8.2 m Subaru telescope, revealing two galaxies in the act of merging. We have followed it up with long-slit optical spectroscopy and integral-field unit (IFU) observation with 10.4 m Gran Telescopio Canaris (GTC) and 4.2 m William Herschel Telescope (WHT), and a 10 ksec X-ray observatory pointing to study the host galaxy environment of a beamed AGN in an unprecedented detail.

However, the observation of the flux variability, especially in the ray band, and also the detection of a flat GHz frequency spectrum 2018MNRAS.477.5127Y indicates that the radio emission detected from TXS 2116 077 is jet dominated and hence the derived kinematic age is reliable. A comparison of the jet kinematic age ( < 15 kyr) with the merger timescale (0.5 2 Gyr) suggests the jet to be considerably younger than the merger.

sources.summary: The paper reclassifies SDSS J211852.96-073227.5 as an intermediate Seyfert, models it as a late-type AGN host in an interacting system, and links its gamma-ray emission to a relativistic jet.

quotes.quote: Here we present for the first time a detailed spectroscopic and morphological analysis of a Seyfert galaxy, SDSS J211852.96-073227.5, at z = 0.26 . This source has previously been classified as a gamma-ray emitting narrow-line Seyfert 1 galaxy. We re-observed it with the 6.5m Clay Telescope and these new, high-quality spectroscopic data have revealed that it is actually an intermediate-type Seyfert galaxy.

Furthermore, the results of modelling the Ks band near-infrared images obtained with the 6.5m Baade Telescope indicate that the AGN is hosted by a late-type galaxy in an interacting system, strengthening the suggested connection between galaxy interactions and relativistic jets.

The host galaxy of J2118-0732 is clearly interacting with a nearby companion galaxy. The morphology of the system is already quite disturbed, but based on our modelling, both galaxies have pseudo-bulges, indicating that both of them had late-type morphologies before the encounter.

sources.summary: The paper models TXS 2116--077 as a merging Seyfert galaxy whose central SMBH can form by baryon-induced dark matter core collapse within the merger timescale.

quotes.quote: An interesting case is the Seyfert galaxy TXS 2116--077, which is merging with another nearby Seyfert galaxy on an estimated timescale of 0.5 - 2 Gyr. It hosts a radio jet with a kinematic age shorter than 15 kyr, considerably shorter than the merger timescale, strengthening the above hypothesis 2020ApJ...892..133P . The derived mass of the central SMBH of TXS 2116--077 is M_ BH 3 10^7 M_ , which agrees with the example presented in Fig. fig:mrhoplane (endpoint of black-dashed curve; blue curve in Fig. fig:tBH ) .

The initial dark matter core of M_ dm 2 10^7 M_ , accreting baryonic matter of density _b = 1 M_ pc ^ 3 moving at 200 km s ^ 1 , so Q _b = 0.125 , collapses forming such an SMBH in t_ BH 0.6 Gyr ; see Eqs. ( eq:tau )--( eq:tcrit ) and Fig. fig:tBH . These baryonic matter conditions agree with the TXS 2116--077 inner region observations 2020ApJ...892..133P , for a Compton thick active galactic nucleus with typical column density N_H=10^ 24 cm ^ 2 . It is worth recalling that the present theory predicts the core is surrounded by a dark matter halo of M_h 10^ 11 M_ (see Fig. fig:profile ), in agreement with the TXS 2116--077 total mass 2020ApJ...892..133P .

sources.summary: The review describes TXS 2116 077 as a newly gamma-detected NLSy1 with rapid infrared variability and visible low-state accretion-disc emission.

quotes.quote: With the new Pass 8 event-level analysis (e.g., atwood13 ) and by using 7 and 8.5 years of Fermi LAT data other two new NLSy1 have been detected for the first time in rays, B3 1441 + 476 and TXS 2116 077, as reported in dammando16a,paliya18 , respectively.
Rapid infrared variability over a time-scale consistent with the jet-emitting region has been observed also in TXS 2116 077 by WISE yang18 .

The accretion disc emission is visible in the low activity state of the SED of PMN J0948 + 0022 dammando15b , 1H 0323 + 342 abdo2009b , FBQS J1644 + 2619 larsson18 , TXS 2116 077 yang18 , and PKS 1502 + 036 dammando16b .

gmw_00003069.name: RGB J0152+017
gmw_00003069.overview: RGB J0152+017 is a high-frequency-peaked BL Lacertae object located at a redshift of z=0.08 [arXiv:1603.02410]. This active galactic nucleus has been recognized as a very-high-energy emitter since 2008 [arXiv:1603.02410].

sources.summary: The paper treats RGB J0152+017 as an HBL VHE emitter, noting VERITAS observations across three seasons and an upper limit consistent with the previous detection.

quotes.quote: RGB J0152+017 (HBL, z=0.08 ) This source has been known as a VHE emitter since 2008 0152HESS , when it was detected by \ at a flux of 2.6 Crab above 240 GeV, during 15 hours of observation. \ observed the blazar in three different seasons: 2007-2008 (covering the \ period), 2010-2011 and 2011-2012. The \ upper limit ( 3.6 Crab above 240 GeV) is fully consistent with the \ detection.

gmw_00003070.name: RGB J1725+118
gmw_00003070.overview: RGB J1725+118 is a BL Lacertae object [arXiv:1603.06523, arXiv:1606.06431] classified as a high-frequency peaked blazar [arXiv:1603.02410]. It is a faint radio source that exhibits large-amplitude optical outbursts [arXiv:1606.06431] and very high energy gamma-ray emission [arXiv:1603.06523, arXiv:1603.02410].

sources.summary: MAGIC observed H1722+119 as a BL Lac object and first detected it in the VHE gamma-ray band, estimated its redshift from Fermi data, and modeled its unusual SED with a helical jet SSC model.

quotes.quote: The Major Atmospheric Gamma-ray Imaging Cherenkov (MAGIC) telescopes observed the BL Lac object H1722+119 (redshift unknown) for six consecutive nights between 2013 May 17 and 22, for a total of 12.5 h.

The source was for the first time detected in the very high energy (VHE, E > 100 GeV) ray band with a statistical significance of 5.9 . The integral flux above 150 GeV is estimated to be (2.0 0.5) per cent of the Crab Nebula flux. We used contemporaneous high energy (HE, 100 MeV < E < 100 GeV) ray observations from Fermi LAT (Large Area Telescope) to estimate the redshift of the source. Within the framework of the current extragalactic background light models, we estimate the redshift to be z = 0.34 0.15 .

The spectral energy distribution (SED) of H1722+119 shows surprising behaviour in the 3 10^ 14 10^ 18 Hz frequency range. It can be modelled using an inhomogeneous helical jet synchrotron self-Compton model.

sources.summary: The paper describes H 1722+118 as a VHE BL Lac source in its monitoring sample and reports its optical/radio flux level, variability trends, and correlation behavior.

quotes.quote: H 1722+118 : Faint radio source with large amplitude optical outbursts. Visual inspection suggests increasing trend in the radio light curve starting around MJD 56000 and decreasing before it, resulting in no significant trend in our trend analysis (see Fig. Fig:lc25 ). There is a significant correlation between the two bands, and in the absence of flares in the radio light curve, it seems to be a result of slow variability common in the two bands. However, the polynomial subtraction does not decrease the rms of the optical light curve significantly.

sources.summary: The paper describes RGB J1725+118 as an HBL discovered in VHE during an elevated optical state, with a VERITAS upper limit consistent with that measurement.

quotes.quote: RGB J1725+118 (HBL, z > 0.35 ) The discovery of VHE emission from this blazar was recently reported by \ Magic1725 at a flux of 2 Crab above 140 GeV during observations in May 2013 triggered by an elevated optical state. No spectral information is available at the present time. The \ upper limit ( 3.2 Crab above 200 GeV) is consistent with the \ measurement.

gmw_00003071.name: 0FGL J2001.0+4352
gmw_00003071.overview: 0FGL J2001.0+4352 is a candidate high-frequency peaked BL Lacertae object located at a redshift of approximately 0.18 [arXiv:1603.02410]. The blazar exhibits variable very-high-energy emission, characterized by a bright single-night flare and a lower baseline flux [arXiv:1603.02410].

sources.summary: The paper describes 0FGL J2001.0+4352 as an HBL candidate later detected in a bright single-night flare, while VERITAS limits indicate variable VHE emission.

quotes.quote: 0FGL J2001.0+4352 (HBL, z = 0.18: ) Early results from \ indicated that this source was a good candidate for IACTs, in particular due to its hard GeV spectrum Fermi0FGL . The \ collaboration detected the source at a flux of 10 Crab during a single night 1.4 hours on July 16, 2010, see Magic2001 . The \ upper limit clearly indicates that this blazar is variable at VHE, and that its baseline flux is below 5.2 Crab above 200 GeV.

gmw_00003072.name: RGB J2243+203
gmw_00003072.overview: RGB J2243+203 is a blazar and BL Lacertae object [arXiv:1508.07251, arXiv:1701.04305] with an uncertain redshift estimated to be at least 0.22 [arXiv:1701.04305]. The active galactic nucleus exhibits variable very-high-energy gamma-ray emission [arXiv:1603.02410], which was detected by VERITAS during a flare in December 2014 [arXiv:1709.05403, arXiv:1510.01269].

sources.summary: The paper reports VERITAS target-of-opportunity observations of RGB J2243+203, a Fermi LAT BL Lac with uncertain redshift, detecting VHE emission during a monitored flare.

quotes.quote: RGB J2243+203 is a Fermi LAT-detected BL Lac object with unknown redshift. It has been classified as an IBL and an HBL, and is probably a borderline case. The favorable extrapolation of its hard LAT spectrum ( _ 2FGL 1.75 , _ 1FHL 2.4 ) motivated 5 h of observation of this object by VERITAS in 2009, but it was not detected, yielding a flux limit of 2 Crab. Although previous observations were unsuccessful, this object was one of hundreds of candidate VHE emitters whose Fermi LAT flux and photon index is automatically monitored on timescales ranging from 0.25 - 28 days by VERITAS.
An excess of 200 rays ( 6 ) was detected from RGB J2243+203. The observed flux above 160 GeV is 6 Crab and the observed photon index is = 4.6 0.6 .

sources.summary: The paper finds RGB J2243+203 featureless in the observed optical range, cannot test a previously claimed 3900 Å feature, and sets z>0.22.

quotes.quote: RGB J2243+203 : laurent1998 presents the first optical spectrum of this source, found featureless. Similarly the spectrum obtained by shaw2013a is featureless but the authors claimed the detection of an absorption line at 3900 identified as Mg II (2800 ). If confirmed this would imply a redshift z > 0.395. Our spectrum, that does not cover the 3900 region, is very featureless from 4100 to 9000 with the limits on the emission or absorption lines of EW _ min < 0.2. This corresponds to a lower limit of z > 0.22.

sources.summary: The paper reports VERITAS discovery of very-high-energy gamma rays from RGB J2243+203, associated multiwavelength fluxes, X-ray observations, and EBL-dependent redshift limits.

quotes.quote: Very-high-energy (VHE; > 100 GeV) gamma-ray emission from the blazar RGB J2243+203 was discovered with the VERITAS Cherenkov telescope array, during the period between 21 and 24 December 2014. The VERITAS energy spectrum from this source can be fit by a power law with a photon index of 4.6 0.5 , and a flux normalization at 0.15 TeV of (6.3 1.1) 10^ 10 cm ^ 2 s ^ 1 TeV ^ 1 . The integrated Fermi LAT flux from 1 GeV to 100 GeV during the VERITAS detection is (4.1 0.8) 10^ 8 cm ^ 2 s ^ 1 , which is an order of magnitude larger than the four-year-averaged flux in the same energy range reported in the 3FGL catalog, ( 4.0 0.1 10^ 9 cm ^ 2 s ^ 1 ). The detection with VERITAS triggered observations in the X-ray band with the Swift XRT. However, due to scheduling constraints Swift XRT observations were performed 67 hours after the VERITAS detection, not simultaneous with the VERITAS observations.

sources.summary: The paper reports RGB J2243+203 as a VERITAS VHE detection with 5.6 sigma significance, unknown redshift constrained photometrically above 0.39, and targeting prompted by increased Fermi-LAT flux.

quotes.quote: The last six months have been particularly active for northern gamma-ray blazars, resulting in several new VERITAS detections of potentially interesting objects: PKS 1441+25, RGB J2243+203, and S3 1227+25.

RGB J2243+203 was observed for 280 minutes between Dec. 21st and Dec. 24th, 2014, resulting in a cumulative significance of 5.6 . The redshift of this object is unknown (photometrically estimated at z >0.39 ) but it was identified by Fermi LAT as a good candidate for VHE observation in their hard source catalog, 1FHL J2244.0+2020 . VERITAS has an active program to monitor the Fermi LAT data of known and potential VHE emitters and this source was targeted for observations after it exhibited a flux increase in the 1-100 GeV band.

sources.summary: The paper describes RGB J2243+203 as an HBL detected in a 2014 flare, with older VERITAS limits showing much lower VHE emission and hence variability.

quotes.quote: RGB J2243+203 (HBL, z>0.39 ) VHE emission from this source was detected by \ during December 2014, following a trigger from a high \ flux 2243veritas, 2243icrc . Preliminary analysis indicates that the flux from the flaring blazar was at 4 Crab above 180 GeV. The upper limits computed from 2009 observations indicate that the VHE emission from this source is variable, being significantly lower (2.1 Crab above 170 GeV) than the 2014 detection.

gmw_00003073.name: B3 2247+381
gmw_00003073.overview: B3 2247+381 is a high-frequency peaked BL Lacertae object located at a redshift of z=0.119 [arXiv:1603.02410]. This blazar exhibits variability in its simultaneous X-ray light curve and has been detected in a high optical flux state [arXiv:1603.02410].

sources.summary: The paper describes B3 2247+381 as an HBL detected during an optically high state, while VERITAS non-detection constrains its lower VHE state.

quotes.quote: B3 2247+381 (HBL, z=0.119 ) This source was detected by \ in 14 hours of observations from September to October 2010, at a flux of 2.2 Crab above 170 GeV 2247magic . The \ observations were triggered by a high optical state, and there is evidence of variability in the simultaneous X-ray light curve. \ observations do not cover the \ detection, nor the high optical flux state measured by the Tuorla observatory. The non-detection by \ (flux upper limit equal to 1.8 Crab above 170 GeV) constrains the low-state flux of this blazar to be lower than the \ detection, suggesting that it may have been related to a VHE high-flux state.

gmw_00003074.name: 1ES 0037+405
gmw_00003074.overview: 1ES 0037+405 is an active galactic nucleus classified as a blazar [arXiv:1603.02410]. Specifically, it is a high-frequency peaked BL Lacertae object with an unknown redshift [arXiv:1603.02410].

sources.summary: The paper notes 1ES 0037+405 was observed after a VERITAS hotspot near M31, but follow-up did not confirm it and the cumulative significance stayed low.

quotes.quote: 1ES 0037+405 (HBL, z unknown) \ observations of this target were taken as a self-triggered ToO. During observations of the Andromeda galaxy M31, see Bird15 , a 4 hotspot coincident with this blazar was observed in the reconstructed sky-map. However, further observations did not confirm the hotspot, and the cumulative significance is 1.5 in 36 hours.
